preFix.title: Building a simple step unit for a deck or raised floor
preFix.subtitle: Construct a single or double-step unit from treated softwood
preFix.excerpt: How to build a basic step unit to bridge a deck or raised floor to ground level: calculating tread depth and riser height, cutting the side stringers, fixing the treads, and securing the unit so it cannot shift in use.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A step unit for a deck or raised platform needs only two components: a pair of shaped

boards. Risers (the vertical boards closing each step front) are usually omitted on outdoor steps to allow water and debris to pass through. Use tanalised softwood throughout for any outdoor installation.

Measure the total rise: the vertical distance from ground level to the finished deck or floor surface. Divide by the number of steps required; each riser height should fall between 150 mm and 200 mm for a comfortable outdoor step. The tread depth should be at least 250 mm. Mark both dimensions on the end grain of the stringer board, using the combination square to keep lines square.

Set out the step profile on one stringer board using the tape measure and combination square: mark the horizontal tread cut and the vertical riser cut at each step in sequence. Crosscut each notch carefully and check the profile with the square. Use this first stringer as a template to mark and cut the second, so both sides match exactly.

Stand both stringers upright in position, checking they are parallel and the tread notches are level with the spirit level. Cut the tread boards to length; the tread should overhang the stringer face by 20 to 25 mm at the front. Drive two 65 mm screws through the tread into each stringer end. Sand the tread front edges with the sanding block to remove splinters; leave the tread surface unsanded so it gives grip in the wet.

Fix the top of the step unit to the deck or platform frame using 80 mm screws through the top of the stringers into the rim joist. At the base, drive 300 mm timber stakes into the ground behind the lower stringer and screw the stringers to the stakes. This prevents the unit from kicking forward under foot load.
What it looks like when it is right

All treads are level side to side. The unit does not rock when a person stands on the top step. Each tread overhangs evenly at front and both sides. The riser height is consistent from step to step; an uneven step in the middle of a flight is a trip hazard.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
